responses: We built an AI-powered demand forecasting system for a regional grocery distribution network covering 340 retail locations. The system ingests point-of-sale data, promotional calendars, weather forecasts, and local event data to generate 14-day forward demand forecasts at the SKU-location level. The problem being solved is that our prior forecasting model — a seasonal ARIMA with manual promotional adjustments — consistently underforestcasted demand spikes by 23% and overforestcasted during slow periods by 18%, resulting in both stockouts and excess inventory. Over 12 months, the AI forecasting system has reduced stockout rate from 4.2% to 1.8% and reduced days-of-supply at overstocked locations from 8.2 to 5.1, with an estimated $4.2M annual reduction in waste and expediting costs.

We evaluated five approaches: ARIMA with improved feature engineering, gradient boosted trees, an LSTM sequence model, a transformer-based time-series model, and a hybrid ensemble. We rejected ARIMA because its architecture cannot natively capture the non-linear interactions between promotional timing, weather, and local events that dominate demand variance. Gradient boosted trees performed well on static features but required extensive feature engineering to capture temporal dependencies. The LSTM showed strong performance on products with stable demand patterns but struggled with irregular demand spikes from promotions. The transformer model was the highest-performing single model but required 3x the compute for inference and had a longer training cycle that made weekly retraining impractical. We chose a gradient boosted tree ensemble for the majority of SKUs with a transformer overlay for high-velocity SKUs where accuracy gains justified the cost. The explicit tradeoff we accepted is operational complexity — maintaining two model types requires more engineering capacity and creates potential for divergent model behavior during transitions.

We identified four failure modes with associated mitigation and containment strategies. First: forecast collapse for new SKUs with no history. Mitigation: new SKUs are bootstrapped using category-level averages for the first 8 weeks, with tighter reorder triggers that compensate for forecast uncertainty; the system flags these SKUs explicitly in the dashboard. Second: feature pipeline failure — missing weather or event data causing silent model degradation. Mitigation: all input features have freshness monitors with automated alerts; the system falls back to a simpler model when feature freshness checks fail. Third: distribution center assignment changes cause the model to receive data from different retail clusters, invalidating location-level patterns. Mitigation: the system monitors input distribution drift using PSI scores; when drift exceeds threshold, the affected SKU-location pairs are flagged and retrained on a priority basis. Fourth: promotional lift overestimation for new promotional formats we have not run before. Mitigation: first-run promotions are capped at a conservative lift factor until we have actuals; this is enforced in the ordering logic, not the model.

The most significant learning was that model accuracy metrics measured in development environments were systematically optimistic because our backtesting methodology used data that was available in hindsight but would not have been available at the time of the forecast. We had used the final version of the promotional calendar for backtesting, but in production the promotional calendar changes up to 72 hours before execution. When we rebuilt the backtesting framework to simulate information availability at forecast time, our error metrics increased by 30%, which was still better than the prior system but significantly less impressive than the initial claims. We now require all model evaluations to use a forward-only backtesting protocol. The AI model also confidently produced zero-demand forecasts for a product category during a period when we had a data pipeline issue that removed recent sales — it treated missing data as zero sales. We added explicit missing-data handling and imputation to the pipeline after this incident, and documented it as a required check in our model deployment checklist.

rationale: Strong L4 with weaker blast-radius, reflection, and override evidence. Blast radius scores 3 because impact scope (340 locations, stockout/overstock consequences) is identified but there is no quantified containment strategy for worst-case scenarios. Reflection scores 3 because lessons are specific and connected to process changes but do not show systemic evolution in methodology. Override evidence scores 3 because one specific AI error is described with a fix, without a broader pattern of critical evaluation.
